---
title: "Rwasmtime API boundaries"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Rwasmtime API boundaries}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(collapse = TRUE, comment = "#>")
```

# API grammar

`Rwasmtime` is pipe-first. The canonical grammar is:

```r
runtime <- wt_runtime_spec() |>
  wt_with_compiler("cranelift", opt_level = "speed") |>
  wt_enable_features(component_model = TRUE, simd = TRUE) |>
  wt_build_runtime()

wasi <- wt_wasi() |>
  wt_wasi_stdio(stdin = "empty", stdout = "capture", stderr = "capture")

limits <- wt_limits() |>
  wt_limit_memory("512MiB") |>
  wt_limit_wall_time(ms = 5000)

callbacks <- wt_callbacks() |>
  wt_add_callback(
    name = "rwasmtime:host/callbacks.log",
    fun = function(msg) message(msg),
    params = list(msg = "string"),
    results = NULL
  )

plugin <- wt_app("plugin.component.wasm") |>
  wt_as_component() |>
  wt_with_runtime(runtime) |>
  wt_with_wasi(wasi) |>
  wt_with_limits(limits) |>
  wt_with_callbacks(callbacks) |>
  wt_prepare()
```

Composition verbs return the same conceptual object. Terminal verbs return
results, jobs, arrays, or memory reads.

# Boundaries

The Rust core owns Wasmtime runtime state and never calls the R API. The R/Savvy
adapter owns R functions, conditions, protection, and main-thread evaluation.
Callbacks from Wasm are not direct calls into R. They are request/reply messages
through a callback broker.

```text
Wasmtime worker or async host function
  -> build CallbackRequest
  -> schedule it through the adapter callback bridge
  -> for non-void callbacks, wait on native synchronization for CallbackReply

R/Savvy adapter on the R main thread
  -> wake through a POSIX input handler or Windows message pump
  -> evaluate the registered R closure
  -> serialize/rebuild CallbackReply
  -> signal the waiting worker when a reply is required
```

Normal user code should not have to call a drain function. The intended backend
mirrors the Rtinycc pattern: callbacks posted from worker-capable code are
serviced automatically by platform event machinery, and generated/bound wrappers
hide any main-thread pumping needed while a worker is running. `wt_drain_callbacks()`
remains only as an advanced hook for adapter tests or unusual tight native loops
that cannot yield to R's event loop.

# WASI authority

WASI is not implied by loading a guest. The guest receives no ambient filesystem,
environment, stdio, random, clock, or network authority unless a `wt_wasi()`
object grants it.

# Arrays and memory

Use component values for small structured values. Use explicit linear-memory
buffers for large numeric vectors, matrices, and tensors.

```r
# Core-module guests can use the implemented copied linear-memory path.
session <- core_plugin |> wt_new_session()

xbuf <- session |>
  wt_array_write(x, dtype = "f64", layout = "column-major")

ans <- session |>
  wt_call("fit", xbuf)
```

The default implementation must copy data. Borrowed memory views must be explicit
about lifetime and mutability. Component-value array transport remains a future
WIT/value-conversion boundary.

# Structural binding metadata

Core Wasm modules are self-describing at the structural ABI level. Native
compiled artifacts can expose this with `wt_imports()`, `wt_exports()`, and
`wt_bindings()`:

```r
artifact <- runtime |>
  wt_compile("plugin.wasm", kind = "module")

imports <- artifact |> wt_imports()
exports <- artifact |> wt_exports()
bindings <- artifact |> wt_bindings()
```

This metadata is intentionally shallow. It can report that an export is
`(i32, i32) -> i32`, that a module imports `env::memory`, or that a table is
`funcref` with a declared minimum. It cannot infer whether an `i32` is an R
integer, a guest pointer, a string offset, an array handle, or a resource token.
Those semantics must come from WIT/component metadata or an explicit ABI policy
provided by the user or package author.

# Component and WIT values

Component calls cross a copied-value boundary. Small structured arguments should
be represented as WIT/component values; large numeric payloads should use explicit
linear-memory or arena-backed array transport. The Rust core must not receive R
objects, `SEXP`, ALTREP, or borrowed R memory as WIT values. The Savvy adapter is
responsible for converting R inputs into owned Rust value trees and converting
owned results back to R.

# AOT and compiler choices

AOT is an artifact layer, not a separate API family. Core-module AOT is the
implemented native artifact path today:

```r
artifact <- runtime |>
  wt_compile("plugin.wasm", kind = "module") |>
  wt_aot_save("plugin.cwasm", overwrite = TRUE)
```

Component AOT remains a future boundary. Every AOT artifact must include
compatibility metadata: Wasmtime version, target, compiler strategy,
optimization level, enabled features, component model state, and Rwasmtime
artifact format version.
